title: SEGMENT 2 — Where GFP came from: Shimomura and the jellyfish

lines.text: SEGMENT 2 — Where GFP came from: Shimomura and the jellyfish
The story begins, more or less, with a jellyfish.
In 1960, a young Japanese chemist named Osamu Shimomura moved from Nagoya to America to work with a biologist at Princeton on a glowing animal.
The animal was the crystal jelly, Aequorea victoria, a small translucent jellyfish that lives in the cold waters off the Pacific Northwest of the United States.
When you bump it, the rim of its bell flashes with a beautiful blue-green light.
Shimomura's question was: how does it do that?
What's the chemistry of the flash?
Now, to answer that question, Shimomura needed protein.
A lot of protein.
And the only source was the jellyfish itself.
So every summer, for many years, Shimomura and his family and his colleagues drove to Friday Harbor in Washington State, and they collected jellyfish.
By hand.
They used buckets and nets.
They scooped them out of the water, cut the glowing rings off the bell — because only the rim contains the light-producing tissue — and processed those rings to extract the proteins inside.
I want you to picture the scale of this.
Over the entire run of the work, by some estimates, Shimomura's team collected something like eight hundred and fifty thousand jellyfish.
Nearly a million animals.
By hand.
Decades of summers spent in the cold water of the Pacific, year after year, all to feed a biochemistry project whose payoff was, at that point, completely unknown.
That is the patience this discovery cost.
If you take nothing else from the history, take the image of that man with a bucket, scooping jellyfish, summer after summer.
What did he find?
Two things, and both of them mattered.
First, he found a protein he named aequorin.
Aequorin is what we call a bioluminescent protein.
It actually produces light by a chemical reaction — when it binds calcium ions, in the presence of a small co-factor molecule, it emits a flash of blue light.
Aequorin would later become, in its own right, one of the first calcium indicators biologists ever had.
We'll come back to calcium sensing much later in this lecture, because the descendants of that original idea are now the workhorse tools of modern neuroscience.
But here is the puzzle.
Aequorin glows blue.
The jellyfish flashes green.
So there had to be something else, some second molecule, that converted the blue light into the green light you actually see in the water.
Shimomura looked in his preparations, and he found it.
There was a second protein, separate from aequorin, that didn't do any chemistry of its own.
It didn't need a substrate.
It didn't need an enzyme.
You just shone light at it and it glowed green.
He called it the green protein.
We now call it green fluorescent protein, or GFP.
Think about what this second protein is, because it's the strange and important part.
Every other glowing system anyone knew at the time required machinery — an enzyme acting on a small molecule, a chemical reaction.
Here was a protein that contained its glow-producing machinery inside itself, built entirely out of its own amino acids, with no help from anything else.
Hand it light, get back light.
That had never been seen before.
It was, biochemically speaking, a one-piece molecular glow stick.
What was happening inside the jellyfish was actually a beautiful little relay.
Aequorin, triggered by a calcium spike, made blue light.
That blue light didn't escape into the water; it was immediately captured by a nearby GFP molecule, which absorbed the blue and re-emitted it as green.
So the jellyfish was running an optical two-protein relay, and the green flash you see in the water is GFP's emission, not aequorin's.

We'll come back to that kind of energy hand-off in Segment 10, when we talk about FRET — because the same principle, two glow sticks whispering to each other across nanometers, becomes the basis for an entire family of biological sensors.
But for our story, the important thing in the 1960s is that Shimomura had isolated this strange standalone green protein.
He described it in 1962.
And then it sat in the literature, more or less, for thirty years.
Nobody saw the application.
Nobody yet understood that this little protein from a jellyfish would, one day, become the most important molecular tool in all of cell biology.
For that to happen, you needed the gene.
And the gene wasn't cloned until 1992.
The person who cloned it was a careful scientist named Douglas Prasher, working at Woods Hole.
Prasher saw the potential.
He understood, more or less correctly, that if you had the gene, you could in principle put it into any organism and that organism's cells could now make GFP.
But his lab ran out of funding.
His career path took some hard turns.
He sent the cloned gene off to a couple of other labs and stepped away from active science for a long stretch.

There's a melancholy detail to this part of the story: Prasher worked, for a period, driving a courtesy van at a car dealership, while the gene he cloned went on to revolutionize biology and earn other people Nobel Prizes.
Science is a relay race, and not every leg gets the trophy.
The second the gene got into the hands of someone who saw what to do with it, the whole field exploded.
That brings us to the next segment.
